failure_surface: Test case validation returned 0, indicating the output does not match expected results despite successful code execution.
root_cause: The agent misinterpreted the target range scope. The instructions specified writing scores to cells B5:E7 (3 rows × 4 columns), but the agent wrote all calculated values exclusively to column B and cleared columns C, D, E in each target row. The expected behavior requires distributing one person's score across all four columns per row, or correctly mapping each person to their designated column position.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before implementing cell-level writes, explicitly parse the full target range dimensions (rows AND columns) and ensure write operations populate all required cells rather than collapsing multi-column output into a single column.
evidence: Target range specified as 'B5:E7' in instruction metadata (answer_position: 'B5:E7')
Solution code writes only to column 2: ws.cell(row=row_num, column=2, value=float(pts))
Solution code clears remaining columns: for c in range(3, 6): ws.cell(row=row_num, column=c, value=''))
Code executed without runtime error ('File saved successfully'), yet test_case_results shows 0 failures
Multiple syntax errors occurred initially due to malformed code block submission
points_list contains exactly 3 values (one per person from rows 1-3), but target range covers 3×4=12 cells

memory_items.title: Target Range Dimension Parsing
memory_items.lesson: When an instruction specifies a cell range (e.g., A1:D10), extract both row and column boundaries to determine the total number of cells that must be populated.
memory_items.generalization: Always validate that write operations cover the full extent of the specified target range, not just its leading edge or single dimension.

memory_items.title: Multi-Cell Write Pattern Verification
memory_items.lesson: If data must be written across multiple rows and columns, confirm whether values should repeat horizontally, distribute by source entity, or follow another pattern defined in the instructions.
memory_items.generalization: Before executing batch writes, map out how input items (people, records) correspond to output cells to avoid collapsing multi-dimensional outputs into single-column patterns.

memory_items.title: Execution Syntax Hygiene
memory_items.lesson: Repeated syntax errors during code submission consume execution budget and may indicate environment handling issues beyond pure logic errors.
memory_items.generalization: Verify code block formatting and marker usage before execution attempts to prevent wasted iterations on preventable syntactic issues.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Target Range Comprehension
When a task specifies a multi-dimensional cell range (e.g., rows X-Y, columns A-B), parse BOTH row AND column boundaries before planning write operations. Do not collapse multi-column outputs into a single column unless explicitly instructed.

### Value-to-Cell Mapping Strategy
Before writing computed values, establish a clear mapping rule: Which input item corresponds to which output cell? When output spans multiple columns per row, verify whether values should distribute across columns or align with specific column positions.

### Batch Write Coverage Validation
After implementing loop-based cell writes, verify that the iteration scheme covers the FULL target range (total_rows × total_columns). Missing cells or overwriting unintended cells indicates incorrect loop bounds or column index calculations.
